pattern: Whether a run of text can be turned into a mirror image by dropping exactly one character comes down to finding where its two outer ends first fail to agree, then checking whether either side of that failure is already a mirror on its own.
hints: Checking 'is this substring already a palindrome' is only half the picture -- you also need a way to test whether dropping exactly one character from anywhere inside it can repair a near-miss, and doing that fresh for every one of the O(n^2) substrings from scratch is too slow.

Scan a candidate substring from its two outer ends inward, one step at a time; as long as the characters agree keep moving in, but the instant they disagree, the only two characters worth removing are the two that just failed to match each other -- try dropping each one in turn and see if what remains, read the same way, agrees all the way to the middle.

Precompute, for every pair of positions, whether the text between them already reads the same forwards and backwards, and separately how many outer layers agree before the first disagreement; with both tables built once (smallest ranges first), every substring can then be judged in constant time by looking up whether either side of its own first disagreement is already a mirror.

edge_inputs.description: single mismatch fixable by dropping the character between the two ends
already a full palindrome of even length
the whole string fails, but dropping the leading extra character rescues a shorter run
uniform run of one repeated letter
all distinct letters -- no single removal can rescue anything longer than two characters
near-palindrome broken by one interior character
an inner mirrored block sits off to one side of the string rather than centered
longer mixed string with several nested near-matches

stress.description: fully random lowercase text near the maximum length, exercising the general case at scale
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 2500))
    letters = 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz'
    s = ''.join(rng.choice(letters) for _ in range(n))
    return [s]

stress.description: text drawn from a two-letter alphabet, producing dense overlapping near-palindromes to stress the layered mismatch bookkeeping
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 2500))
    letters = 'ab'
    s = ''.join(rng.choice(letters) for _ in range(n))
    return [s]
stress.note: a tiny alphabet maximizes the number of substrings that are palindromes or one edit away, exercising the busiest cases for the mismatch-depth table
